# Weekly Sector Rotation Commentary: 2026-07-12

**⚠️ Not financial advice.** This commentary is auto-generated each week by Anthropic's Claude (an AI model). Brian Beals is not a registered investment advisor, and Anthropic's Claude is not licensed to provide personalized financial advice. The screener is a research and methodology demo, not a recommendation system. Past performance does not predict future results. Do your own research before making any investment decisions.

[View the dashboard ↗](dashboard.html)

---

## What the screen said this week

The screen classified the current environment as mid-cycle, driven by industrial production (INDPRO) rising 1.7% year over year, which sits inside the 0.0% to 4.0% band the rule set treats as steady expansion. The yield curve reading of +0.38 (the spread between longer and shorter term rates being positive) reinforced that classification. In this rule set, mid-cycle conditions award full cycle-fit scores to sectors like Technology and Communications.

Technology (XLK) topped the composite at 82.8 and was the only Buy signal this week. It scored across the board: strong seasonality (85.7), maximum cycle fit (100.0), and the highest relative strength (RS) in the table at 61.2, with a three-month RS of +19.60%. Relative strength here measures how a sector has performed against the broad market recently. Communications (XLC) placed second at 73.5 but was flagged Watch, meaning its cycle fit was high (100.0) while its relative strength (37.1) fell below the confirmation threshold of 45. No sector triggered an Avoid signal this week, though Healthcare (XLV) sat lowest at 42.2, just above the Avoid line of 40.

## Things worth noticing

The gap between XLK and everything else is the standout feature. XLC shares Technology's perfect cycle-fit score but has a deeply negative three-month RS of -13.37%, which is why it lands in Watch rather than Buy. That is the exact divergence the Watch category is built to capture: the cycle rule favors the sector, but price action has not confirmed.

Seasonality scores are high almost everywhere, from Real Estate's 89.6 down to Energy's 62.3, so seasonality is not doing much to separate the rankings this week. The heavier differentiators are cycle fit and relative strength. Note the "thin sample" tag on XLC's seasonality: that flag means fewer historical observations back that number, so it deserves extra skepticism.

Also worth noting: nearly every sector outside XLK shows negative three-month relative strength, yet the composite still ranks them, since cycle fit and seasonality can prop up a score even when recent price action is weak.

## Methodology reminder

The composite is the weighted sum of Seasonality (25%), Cycle Fit (40%), and Relative Strength (35%). Lookahead bias in the backtest is controlled using FRED ALFRED point-in-time vintages, so each week only uses data that was actually available then. The backtest figure since May 2011 is a property of this specific rule set, not a forecast of anything ahead.
